items.source: Neil Sethi / The Week Ahead + Princeton Chen
items.title: Kevin Warsh Confirmed as Fed Chair — Powell Era Ends Friday
items.idea: Senate votes Monday to confirm Kevin Warsh as the new Federal Reserve Chair. Powell's term ends Friday. Warsh still needs a separate 'election' by the Board of Governors to become FOMC head — Powell's board term technically runs through year-end. First chair transition since 2018. April CPI lands Tuesday and PPI lands Wednesday — Warsh's first data releases as chair-designate, setting the tone for his inflation tolerance immediately.
items.angle: Warsh is hawk-leaning and structuralist. The market will price his stance within 72 hours of confirmation. With CPI Tuesday and PPI Wednesday, whatever public signal Warsh makes this week — even informally — will be amplified. Watch for any deviation from Powell-era 'data dependent' framing.
items.tweetA: Kevin Warsh becomes Fed Chair today. First chair change since 2018. But read the fine print: he still needs the Board of Governors to formally elect him as FOMC head — Powell's board term technically runs through year-end. April CPI lands Tuesday. Warsh's first week in the chair is also his first major data test.
items.tweetB: Powell era ends Friday. 8 years. Zero recessions on his watch (though plenty of inflation). Warsh steps in with CPI data Tuesday and PPI Wednesday. The market will price his inflation tolerance within the first 72 hours. That's the real signal this week — not the data itself, but how the new chair responds to it.

items.source: Neil Sethi / The Week Ahead + Princeton Chen
items.title: Trump-Xi Summit May 14-15 — He Lifeng Runs Technical Trade Talks in Seoul May 12-13
items.idea: Two parallel diplomatic tracks run simultaneously this week: Chinese VP He Lifeng leads a delegation to South Korea May 12-13 for technical US-China trade negotiations. Trump then meets Xi directly May 14-15. This two-track structure — VP-level technical talks followed by summit-level announcement — is the classic pre-deal choreography where the framework is already substantially framed and needs political cover to close.
items.angle: He Lifeng in Seoul is the warm-up act. If Seoul produces framework language, Trump-Xi becomes an announcement, not a negotiation. If Seoul breaks down, the summit is optics. Watch South Korea coverage Monday-Tuesday as the leading signal for what Thursday-Friday produces.
items.tweetA: Two diplomatic tracks simultaneously this week: He Lifeng (China's economic VP) holds US trade talks in Seoul May 12-13. Trump meets Xi May 14-15. This is not coincidence — it's choreography. The Seoul channel locks the technical language. Trump-Xi is where the announcement happens. Watch Seoul output Monday.
items.tweetB: He Lifeng in Seoul Monday-Tuesday. Xi-Trump Thursday-Friday. If you have been waiting for a US-China trade framework to drop, this is the week the architecture gets built. The question is whether Seoul produces a real framework or just talking points. How quickly May 14-15 gets called a 'success' tells you the answer.

items.title: Iran War: 'Large Gaps' on Hormuz + Nuclear — Polymarket 58% Odds No Deal Before June
items.idea: Iran delivered its response to the US peace proposal with 'large gaps' on both the nuclear issue AND control over the Strait of Hormuz. Trump called it 'TOTALLY UNACCEPTABLE.' Polymarket gives 42% odds the sides meet before end of May, 74% by end of June. Neil Sethi's read: 'both sides feel they have the upper hand, and both need a conclusion which allows them to claim victory — not a setup conducive to a quick conclusion.'
items.angle: The Hormuz clause is systematically underreported. Iran demanding to retain maritime leverage over the world's most important oil chokepoint is a structural dealbreaker no US administration can sign. This keeps the war-risk premium in energy alive through at least June, and makes the federal gas tax balloon a symptom of the real problem.
items.tweetA: Iran's peace response: 'large gaps' on BOTH the nuclear issue AND control of the Strait of Hormuz. Trump: 'TOTALLY UNACCEPTABLE.' Polymarket: 58% odds no deal before June. Both sides think they're winning. That math does not resolve quickly. Gas at $4.55 is your baseline through Memorial Day.
items.tweetB: Nobody is talking about the Hormuz clause. Iran is demanding to retain maritime control over the world's most important oil chokepoint as part of a peace deal. No US president can sign that. The gap isn't just nuclear — it's about who controls $5-7T in annual oil trade flows. June timeline is the optimistic scenario.

items.title: Gas at $4.55/gal — 52% Above Pre-War — White House Floats 18.3¢ Federal Tax Holiday
items.idea: National average gas price is $4.55/gal, 52% above pre-Iran-war levels. Energy Secretary Chris Wright says the White House is 'open' to suspending the 18.3¢/gal federal gasoline tax. NY Fed April survey shows 1-year inflation expectations at 3.6%. Even with perfect consumer pass-through, an 18.3¢ suspension is a ~4% price cut on a $4.55 base — and there is no mechanism ensuring that 4% reaches pump prices rather than being absorbed by retailers as margin in a supply-tight market.
items.angle: 4% nominal relief on a 52% price spike is politically required but economically trivial. The real signal is that gas inflation is already baked into 3.6% 1yr inflation expectations. A tax holiday does not lower expectations — it just gives Congress something to vote on before midterms while the Highway Trust Fund bleeds.
items.tweetA: $4.55/gal average gas — 52% above pre-Iran-war level. White House floats suspending the 18.3¢ federal gas tax. Even perfect pass-through = 4% price cut. NY Fed 1yr inflation expectations: 3.6%. You are not solving a 52% spike with a 4% discount on one product. This is midterm optics, not economics.
items.tweetB: The math on the gas tax holiday: 18.3¢ on $4.55 = 4.0% max relief, only if 100% passes through to consumers. In tight supply markets, retailers capture part of that as margin. Meanwhile the 18.3¢ funds the Highway Trust Fund. Actual consumer savings: maybe 2%. Infrastructure funding gap: guaranteed. Great trade.

items.title: Gas Tax Holiday Mechanics: Highway Trust Fund Risk, Congressional Choke Point, Pass-Through Leakage
items.idea: The 18.3¢/gal federal gas tax is statutory — Treasury cannot pause it by memo. Congress must amend the tax code, requiring floor time, offsets, and vote math. The tax funds the Highway Trust Fund. Suspension without a backfill creates an immediate fiscal hole: (a) general-fund transfer, (b) HTF insolvency acceleration, or (c) cuts to state DOT project pipelines. The political play is explicit: White House captures communications benefit while outsourcing the legislative burden and funding gap to Congress entirely.
items.angle: Infrastructure investors and state DOT project pipelines are the hidden casualty. HTF is already under pressure. A suspension without a guaranteed Congressional offset is a slow-motion infrastructure funding crisis wearing a gas-price-relief costume. Key signal: does any bill text specify the HTF backfill mechanism?
items.tweetA: The hidden consequence of a gas tax holiday: the 18.3¢ levy funds the Highway Trust Fund. Suspend without an explicit offset and you get (a) general-fund backfill, (b) HTF insolvency dynamics, or (c) cuts to state DOT project pipelines. An infrastructure funding crisis is hiding inside a gas price headline.
items.tweetB: Watch for bill TEXT, not Sunday-show quotes. The key detail in any gas tax holiday legislation: the HTF backfill mechanism. General-fund transfer? Spending trims? Temporary borrowing? No text = messaging theater. If there IS text, the backfill method tells you who actually pays. Administration takes the headlines; Congress carries the fiscal reality.

items.title: SPX 6 Consecutive Weekly Gains — But Breadth Deteriorating at New Highs
items.idea: Nasdaq +4.5%, SPX +2%, Russell 2000 +2% last week — the 6th consecutive weekly gain for all three, the longest SPX streak since 2024. Breadth is deteriorating at new highs while positioning remains below early-2026 peak levels. The rally is happening on a narrowing base of leaders, classically associated with late-stage momentum concentration where the index advance is supported by fewer and fewer names.
items.angle: Six up weeks with deteriorating breadth is the internal tell. Resolves either via sector rotation (breadth recovers, market widens) or a 5-8% consolidation before the next leg. Positioning still below peaks means retail has not fully re-loaded — that is the remaining fuel for whichever direction wins.
items.tweetA: SPX just posted its 6th consecutive weekly gain — longest streak since 2024. Nasdaq led at +4.5%. But breadth is DETERIORATING at new highs. Fewer stocks carrying the index forward. Classic late-stage concentration signal: either breadth recovers this week or you get a 5-8% consolidation. Positioning below early-2026 peaks = dry powder exists either way.
items.tweetB: The counterintuitive setup: 6 straight up weeks, positioning below peaks, breadth falling. The rally is real, there is still dry powder, but the internal composition is fragile. If you are long, you need to see breadth recover THIS week. If it doesn't, the index can keep climbing while your equal-weight book lags badly.

charts.desc: Charts in subscriber section covering breadth, positioning, sentiment, and seasonality — worth opening full article

items.title: Q1 Earnings Strength 'Relatively Unprecedented' — Not From a Trough, Valuations Held Flat
items.idea: Neil Sethi describes Q1 earnings as 'relatively unprecedented, especially for a quarter not coming out of an earnings trough.' That distinction is load-bearing: prior exceptional earnings growth often benefited from easy comps after a recession or earnings downturn. This cycle has organic incremental profit growth. The result: valuations remained at 'moderate levels' even as equities reached new highs — the multiple did not expand, earnings met the price.
items.angle: This is the structural bull case in one data point. If earnings grow fast enough to keep multiples flat at all-time highs, the valuation bear case has no ground to stand on. Q2 guidance season is the real test — whether management teams guide up, flat, or quietly lower bars.
items.tweetA: Q1 earnings: 'relatively unprecedented for a quarter NOT coming out of an earnings trough.' This matters. It means organic incremental profit growth — not base-effect lapping of weak comps. Result: new SPX highs at moderate valuations. If this pace holds into Q2, the valuation bear case has no floor to stand on.
items.tweetB: If you have been short US equities in 2026 on valuation grounds, here is the sentence that breaks the thesis: 'earnings strength strong enough to keep valuations at moderate levels even as equities hit new highs.' The multiple did not expand — earnings met the price. That is a different market than the one the valuation bears are modeling.

items.source: Neil Sethi / The Week Ahead (citing Deutsche Bank)
items.title: China April PPI: 0.5% → 1.8% YoY Jump Expected; Imports +18% vs Exports +10%
items.idea: Deutsche Bank forecasts China April PPI to surge to 1.8% YoY from 0.5% in March — a 3.5x jump in one month. CPI is expected to tick slightly lower to 0.9% YoY from 1.0%. On trade: DB forecasts exports +10% YoY and imports +18% YoY for April. PPI inflating 3.5x while CPI stagnates signals industrial input cost pressure not yet passed through to consumers — either manufacturer margins are being compressed, or a CPI spike is deferred. Data releases Monday.
items.angle: Import growth nearly double export growth = China absorbing more than it sells externally. PPI surging while CPI stays subdued = domestic industrial heating without consumer inflation. That is a specific combination that matters for commodity-sensitive EM exposure and for assessing Chinese corporate margin health.
items.tweetA: DB forecasting China April PPI to jump from 0.5% to 1.8% YoY — a 3.5x surge in one month. Meanwhile CPI ticks DOWN to 0.9%. Divergence: industrial input inflation not passing through to consumers yet. Either manufacturer margins are getting crushed or a CPI spike is deferred. Data Monday. $FXI $KWEB
items.tweetB: China April trade expected: exports +10% YoY, imports +18% YoY. Import growth nearly double export growth signals domestic absorption running hot. Pair with PPI surging to 1.8% and you have an economy heating up at the industrial level while staying contained at the consumer level. Specific setup for commodity-sensitive EM plays.

items.title: 60/40 Dead Since 2022 — Rayliant Launches CNQQ, a Non-VIE China Internet ETF
items.idea: Gayed's thesis: the market keeps running a playbook that stopped working in 2022 — long-duration US growth as the only trade, a 60/40 that no longer hedges because of positive stock-bond correlation, and 'international' exposure that is really just a different vehicle for the same handful of US-listed mega-caps. Rayliant Global Advisors is launching CNQQ, a China internet ETF explicitly without VIE structure. VIE-structure anxiety has been the primary legal barrier preventing US institutional capital from accessing Chinese internet names at scale.
items.angle: CNQQ is worth monitoring precisely because VIE anxiety has been the single biggest deterrent for US RIAs approaching BABA, PDD, and Tencent. If the non-VIE structure is legally durable, it could unlock a cohort of institutional capital that has been structurally excluded from Chinese internet for years.
items.tweetA: Rayliant launching CNQQ: a China internet ETF with no VIE structure. VIE exposure risk has been the single biggest reason US institutional capital stayed away from BABA, PDD, Tencent. These names are cheap. The question is whether a non-VIE wrapper is legally durable. If it is, significant dry powder gets unlocked.
items.tweetB: Gayed's 3-sentence diagnosis of why most diversified portfolios underperformed benchmarks for 3 years: 'the market keeps running a playbook that stopped working in 2022 — long-duration US growth, a 60/40 that no longer hedges, and international that is just the same tech complex in different clothes.' Hard to argue with that.

items.source: Princeton Chen + Neil Sethi / The Week Ahead
items.title: UK King's Speech Wednesday + Q1 GDP Thursday — Starmer's Full Legislative Agenda
items.idea: UK State Opening of Parliament Wednesday sees the King's Speech announcing Starmer's government legislative programme for 2026. UK Q1 GDP releases Thursday. These two events in sequence provide a picture of where UK fiscal and regulatory policy is heading alongside the actual economic scorecard for Q1 — a check on whether BoE rate cuts are passing through to growth. The UK is operating in a specific regime: cutting rates, attempting fiscal consolidation, and post-Brexit trade dynamics still unresolved.
items.angle: King's Speech is underrated for market signal — items that survive the speech have Treasury backing and political will behind them. Financial services reform, pension reform, and infrastructure investment are the categories with UK equity implications. Thursday GDP tests whether BoE cuts are working.
items.tweetA: Two UK signals this week: King's Speech Wednesday (Starmer's full legislative agenda — watch for infrastructure, financial services, defense signals) and Q1 GDP Thursday. The BoE has been cutting while the government consolidates. Thursday tells us if those two forces are actually producing growth. $EWU
items.tweetB: King's Speech is underrated for market signal. It is the government's public commitment list for the year — the items that survive are the ones with Treasury backing. Financial services reform, pension policy, infrastructure investment are worth scanning for UK equity implications. GDP Thursday is the scorecard.

items.title: 'Saylor to Sell?' — MicroStrategy Bitcoin Disposition Rumor Circulating This Week
items.idea: Seven C Newsletter flags 'Saylor to sell?' as one of the key events of the coming week. No substantive detail in the email body — this is a headline reference to circulating speculation about Michael Saylor / MicroStrategy potentially reducing BTC holdings. MicroStrategy has not sold a satoshi through multiple market cycles. Any actual selling would represent an enormous sentiment and structural shift — MicroStrategy is the single most visible institutional BTC accumulation vehicle, and the 'corporate treasury BTC' playbook has been replicated by many small-cap companies.
items.angle: If MicroStrategy actually disposes, it is not just a price event — it ends the most publicly visible institutional BTC accumulation narrative of the cycle. Watch $MSTR price relative to BTC spot as the leading indicator before any announcement drops. Options market implied vol will also price the probability.
items.tweetA: Circulating this week: 'Saylor to sell?' MicroStrategy hasn't sold a satoshi through multiple cycles. If that changes, it's not just a price event — it ends the most visible institutional BTC accumulation narrative. $MSTR price action relative to BTC spot is your leading indicator before any announcement drops.
items.tweetB: The Saylor-sells rumor: either the biggest BTC macro event of 2026 or pure FUD. Context: MicroStrategy has used every dip as a buying opportunity. Scenarios where Saylor actually sells: (1) forced by balance sheet stress, (2) strategic rebalancing at an internal reserve target, (3) this is false. $MSTR options market prices the probability before headlines confirm.

items.title: AI Trading Workflows Going Mainstream: Vibe Coding TradingView, Claude + Codex as Analyst Agents
items.idea: Seven C Newsletter's weekly 'alpha aggregation' section packages AI trading workflows for its retail-oriented crypto audience: vibe coding custom TradingView indicators, optimizing Claude for market analysis, Hermes as a financial analyst agent, Codex for trading automation, building AI content systems, and deploying scalable stablecoin strategies. The fact that a mainstream retail crypto newsletter is packaging Claude and Codex agent workflows as weekly alpha signals a significant adoption curve milestone.
items.angle: When Claude and Codex agent workflows appear in retail crypto newsletters as 'alpha tips,' the institutional edge from early AI tool adoption has already narrowed to zero. The next competitive moat is workflow design quality and prompt engineering judgment — not tool access. This is the AI equivalent of when retail got Bloomberg terminals.
items.tweetA: Seven C Newsletter — a mainstream retail crypto weekly — is teaching readers to vibe code TradingView indicators, use Claude for market analysis, and build Codex trading agents. AI-native trading workflows have crossed the institutional-to-retail adoption curve. The edge is no longer the tools. It's judgment.
items.tweetB: AI trading tool stack now appearing as retail alpha tips: Claude as analyst, Codex as execution agent, Hermes for portfolio review, stablecoin yield automation. When this is in a weekly retail crypto newsletter, access to the tools is table stakes. Workflow design and prompt quality are the new moat — same dynamic as when everyone got a Bloomberg.
